title: The Gut-Brain Axis: How Your Microbiome Shapes Mood and Cognition
author: RENEW Editorial
excerpt: Your gut and brain are in constant communication through nerves, hormones, and immune signals. Emerging research reveals just how much intestinal health influences mental performance.
text:
The gut-brain axis is a bidirectional communication network linking the central nervous system with the enteric nervous system — sometimes called the "second brain." The vagus nerve carries signals in both directions, while gut bacteria produce neuroactive compounds including serotonin precursors, GABA, and short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) that influence brain function.

Approximately 90% of the body's serotonin is produced in the gut, though it does not directly cross into the brain. Instead, microbial metabolites and immune signaling molecules affect blood-brain barrier integrity, neuroinflammation, and stress hormone regulation. Studies in both animal models and humans link diverse, fiber-rich diets with lower rates of anxiety and depression symptoms.

Dysbiosis — an imbalance in gut microbial communities — has been associated with increased intestinal permeability ("leaky gut"), chronic low-grade inflammation, and altered stress responses. While the field is still evolving, the consensus is clear: gut health is not separate from brain health.
text2:
Practical strategies to support a healthy microbiome include eating 30+ different plant foods per week (the American Gut Project found this associated with greater microbial diversity), consuming fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, and sauerkraut, and prioritizing prebiotic fibers from onions, garlic, oats, and legumes.

Avoid unnecessary antibiotic use, manage chronic stress (which directly alters gut motility and microbial composition), and limit ultra-processed foods that reduce microbial diversity. Probiotic supplements may help specific conditions but are not a substitute for dietary diversity.

The gut-brain connection reminds us that mental wellness is not purely psychological. Nutrition, sleep, and stress management all feed into the same system. Supporting your microbiome is one of the most accessible neuroscience interventions available — no prescription required.

sources.title: Gut–Brain Axis: How the Microbiome Influences Anxiety and Depression
The Neuroactive Potential of the Human Gut Microbiota in Quality of Life and Depression
Psychobiotics and the Gut-Brain Axis: In the Pursuit of Happiness

faq.q: How does the gut microbiome affect mood and mental health?
faq.a: The gut and brain communicate via the gut-brain axis — a bidirectional network involving the vagus nerve, immune signalling, and microbial metabolites. Gut bacteria produce roughly 90% of the body's serotonin, as well as GABA, dopamine precursors, and short-chain fatty acids that directly influence brain function and mood.

faq.q: What foods best support a healthy gut microbiome?
faq.a: Fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut, kombucha) directly introduce beneficial bacteria. High-fibre foods (vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains) feed existing beneficial bacteria. A 2021 Stanford trial found high-fibre and high-fermented food diets both increased microbiome diversity, with fermented foods showing faster results.

faq.q: Can probiotics help with anxiety and depression?
faq.a: Emerging evidence is promising but not yet definitive. Several randomised trials show specific probiotic strains (Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species) reduce anxiety scores and depressive symptoms in healthy and clinical populations. Effects are modest and appear most consistent when gut dysbiosis is also present.

faq.q: How long does it take to change your gut microbiome through diet?
faq.a: Diet changes can alter microbiome composition within 24-48 hours. However, stable, long-term shifts in microbial communities typically require several weeks to months of consistent dietary change. The microbiome generally returns toward its original profile within a few days of reverting to a prior diet.
